paragraphs.text: It Is the Riviera of the Great
Daughter of a Roman General Who Perished a Christian Martyr
Found in 1542, and Explored Just Fifty Years After

Is the Place Where Tired People Come to Soak Up Sunshine—It Is Rich, But Democratic—A Single Grapevine Covers an Acre, Produces Ten Tons of Grapes, Is Eight Feet in Circumference at the Base—Walnuts Yield $200 or More to the Acre.
has 99,463,681 acres. To get a comparative idea you can take the states of
, and spreading them all out together over
there would be an uncovered margin of 13,774 square miles, or more than enough in excess to make more than ten states like
There is more than one outdoor paradise in
. In the list do not fail to include
. The climate has attracted invalids and persons in search of health and pleasure since the earliest settlement of the

, many refined and aristocratic Spanish families made it their home and contributed to the charm of social life. A few descendants of the early Spanish settlers still reside there. They have become thoroughly Americanized, but may easily be identified by their names, their features and complexions and the graceful manners of their race.
, its monastery is the most extensive, it is an educational institution of high standard, and its influence is the widest of any in the state.
the most desirable place of residence for families of wealth and leisure in the
, if not in the world. The climate and environment are very much like those of the
. It is the only place of resort in the
which has a mountain range on one side of it, and an ocean on the other, and where the climate permits the population to live in comfort the year around. It is often called the

of the Pacific, but is more democratic and cosmopolitan than the queen of the Atlanic resorts. No social tyranny is recognized; the habits and customs of the people are not so artificial; everybody lives as nearly as possible to nature: [sic] there is no rivalry in dress or dinner parties, or other forms of display; all classes spend most of their time out-of-doors, and the belle of

shows to the best advantage in a Khaki suit, astride a mustang climbing a mountain trail, rather than at a ball or garden party. The population is made up mostly of "remitters"—that is in this instance people who have retired from business, widows of large means, and others who are able to live luxuriously upon the proceeds of investments elsewhere. Many people from
spent part of the winters in
, owning or renting bungalows, which are of all sizes. There are colonies of people from the big eastern cities of
. The richest resident is a petroleum prince, who has figured in the divorce courts.

and the sea runs the main street of the town, a wide, well-paved thoroughfare, more than two miles long, which is lined on both sides with business blocks, banks, mercantile establishments, and the best bookstores and curio shops to be found on the

. The high class of goods they keep is an index to the wealth and culture of the community. There is a line of trolley cars running from the beach to the mission and a wide and beautiful boulevard follows the shore line for several miles. At the northern end are bath houses, and a bathing pier near a plaza, where a band plays every afternoon. There is sea bathing the year round, and one of the finest bath houses in the land. There are several sanitariums and hot medicinal springs at an elevation of 1,300 feet. One may spend an entire winter in
, and follow a different drive or trail every day.
has so many beautiful drives and trails for motors, carriages and saddle horses. The

is broken by innumerable canyons and gorges, and between them lie broad table lands, which are occupied as ranches, olive groves and walnut orchards. There is a drive or a trail through every canyon, and one of them known as

, twelve miles long, offers a continuous panorama of glorious scenery. It crosses the mountains through a pass a little less than 4,000 feet above the seas. Another trail reaches the summit of the range by a climb of twelve to fourteen miles, and is equally attractive. The lowest pass is 2,224 feet, but it is possible to climb nearly 8,000 either on horseback or by motor.

there is no rivalry in architecture or extravagance. The great competition is in flowers, plants and vegetable gardens. Most of the residences are of the villa type, simple and often severe. The ideal house is of shingles, stained brown, with wide porches hidden by climbing roses and other vines and furnished with hammocks and easy chairs. The fact that the temperature rarely varies more than twelve to fifteen degrees from one year's end to another, and is always moderatley warm, enables everybody to live in the open air, and is, of course, a blessing to the infirm and weary, and has a wholesome influence. People from the east go to
to be soaked in sunshine, and play golf 365 days in the year. The villa population is quite exclusive and has a society of its own. During the winter season the two great hotels, the
, probably as large as the largest on the coast, and one of the finest, and the

, which has recently been erected upon the site of an old house of the same name, are filled with eastern people. During the summer middle west and western people take advantage at the hotels of the lower rates. The
is very close to the ocean, where the sea bathers revel, and the great lawn on that side is in fact a perfect ocean of flowers.
is also attractive. It belongs to
, and he is the man who controlled the contracting company which built the Lackawanna railroad from
about 1880. One of his subcontractors was the late
, who made fame and wealth as the builder of the
in 1880, was in a way, Mr. McDonald's financial backer. The writer saw
, and he looks almost as strong as he did over thirty years ago.

is built of concrete and steel and is after the mission style of architecture, has 300 rooms and cost nearly a half million dollars. The mission idea is carried out in every room and corridor, the woodwork being left in the rough and stained a dark brown by the use of petroleum. There are no ceilings. The heavy rafters are left uncovered; the walls are rough plaster, painted to match the woodwork; the floors are red tiles and even the bath tubs are of mission design.
The mission style of architecture with its strong but simples [sic] lines, [with its ...] Moors and introduced into
because they found it the best suited to the climate. That was the reason it was adopted in the south of

. The brick and tile did not reflect, but absorbed the glare of the sun. The cloisters are cool at noonday and the thick walls are proof agianst the dampness of the rainy season. It is being used more and more for public buildings, school houses, hotels, and nearly all the new schools and public edifices are being built after the mision design. The government architects planned the conventional granite for the federal post-office at
, but to the end that a harmonious architectrure should prevail in the city,
directed that the Riverside post-office should conform to the mission style.
is attractive. The entire amphitheater inclosed by the
on the one side and the

on the other, is entirely taken up by rich ranchmen, who own large estates and keep most of their lands in natural parks. They visit in automobiles and on horseback; they wear sombreros and comfortable clothing; they take pride in their crops and orchards and brag about their walnuts and their olives. This is the walnut country par excellence, and it is the best paying crop. The trees require ten years to mature, but give no further trouble. It is only necessary to keep down the weeds and loosen the surface of the soil with plow and harrow. Walnut trees are not subject to diseases like fruit trees. The nuts require no refrigeration, cannot spoil on the hands of the grower, and sell for not less than twelve cents a pound on the cars. The average profit to the grower is $200 an acre. Walnut orchards are mostly limited in area. The largest in
has only 350 acres, and most of them are of less than twenty acres. But the business is extending rapidly.
The walnut acreage will soon be increased when the entire
is irrigated. Irrigation will enable the farmers to extend the walnut and lemon groves and grow more beans, for these three are great staples.
has been boring a hold through the

for a large and permanent water supply. The tunnel is seven by nine feet in size and 7,000 feet through the mountain. Aqueducts were designed for both sides and the aqueducts' supply of water for drinking, irrigation, etc., will be enough for a population of 250,000 people. No one dreams that
will ever have such a population. The new scheme is to irrigate the
Writing of lemons, it is worth mention that
have an enormous lemon ranch in the
, with a large stone packing house and all the appurtenances and ship their crops by the train load. It is the most extensive and complete lemon ranch in

, with 2,000 acres of trees. The crops are truly wonderful. In this temperature, and in a soil of surpassing richness, everything grows. All the plants known to the temperature and semi-temperate regions live happily together. The palm and the pine, the apple and the oleander[,] the maple and the magnolia grow side by side with equal vigor. Roses bloom and strawberry beds give ripened fruit every month. The cultivation of vegetables and small fruits for the local market, is the most profitable kind of farming, however, as hotels and boarding houses consume large quantities of both. The cultivation of flowers and plants is also a profitable business. On the main street of
is a concern which imports shrubs, vines, plants and trees from everywhere and acclimates them for

. You can see nearly a half hundred kinds of palms, camphor and cinnamon trees, papyrus, bamboos of all sorts and sizes, hibiscus, agavas, bread-fruit, and other strange plants that you read about in Robinson Crusoe, the Swiss Family Robinson and other tales of the
an oil and steel magnate named

has a great show place. It took eight years to construct the palace and surroundings at a cost of more than $3,000,000. It is first of all a palm park. But it is more. The home is copied after some Roman villa, and the grounds likewise. This palace and palm park is open to visitors at certain hours each day when

is away. The house has a great plunge like to the Roman baths. The water runs into high reservoirs, which as they are located along the descending hillside in front are tapped by pipes from which the palms are watered.

has been traveling the world over for many years looking for new varieties of palms[.] He now has about eighty distinct varieties and has sold single plants for as much as $500. A Buffalo man, the head of the
Some of the finest grapes in
the vines having been imported from
in the early days by the
who taught the natives how to make wine as well as olive oil. The largest grape vine in the world, it is said, grows in

. It was planted in 1843 by a Spanish farmer and has yielded ten tons of grapes in a single season. It spreads over an acre of ground and is supported by seventy-eight posts. If it were not pruned so closely every season it would cover a much larger area. Six inches from the ground the trunk measures eight and one-half feet in circumference, and where it reaches the trellis and begins to spread, about six and one-half feet from the ground, the girth is five feet seven inches. There are several other very large vines int he same neighborhood. One of the largest ever known was cut down and sent to the Philadelphia centennial exposition in 1876, as a sample of California products and was a great advertisment for
It will be of interest to all who love the words and music of the song "The Rosary," to know the words were written in
. He is an artist; he lives there and from his studio window there is a most entrancing view. It was set to music by
. Nearby to the scenes of beauty in
one can find the greasy conditions of a rich oil country. Crude petroleum gushes up from the earth and sea at
. It is occupied by a colony of Spiritualists. It looks like one of the old fashioned oil towns of

. The first petroleum was discovered on the hillside, and the veins were followed down to the ocean, where what the Californians call "submarine wells" have been bo[r]ed and piped and are now flowing regularly. The oil comes up first through the sand, and then piped through the water and thence to the reservoirs on shore. The people of

were the first to discover the adaptability of crude petroleum for making roads. It was an accident. The first man to use the crude petroleum on a road was so lazy that he shoveled the refuse from an oil tank into the street to get rid of it, when he should have carted it off to some dumping ground. The oil immediately absorbed the dust, and the heavy metal wheels which passed over it packed it down into a hard, smooth surface like asphaltum which shed the rains and was never muddy or dusty. His neighbors then put oil on the roadways in front of their houses to keep down the dust, and the practice finally became general and is now used effectively in every part of the country[.]
acquired its name from a tragedy, due to a father's killing of his daughter, and after her it was named.
was one of the early martyrs. She was born in
. She was converted to Christianity when she was sixteen years old, in the year A. D. 233, during the reign of
, an emperor frindly to the Christians. His successor,
, was a thorough pagan and ordered all Christian converts put to death. The father of

, and he concealed her, hoping to persuade her to recant. When he was unable to do so he inflicted upon her cruel torments, which she bore with wonderful fortitude for a girl of seventeen, and he finally slew her with his own hand. Her piety, her steadfastness for the faith, and her example had great influence with the young Christians and caused her to be canonized by both the Greek and Roman Catholic churches. She suffered martyrdom at
about 235 A. D. It was not by a monk that
. When the Spaniards first discovered the country they were all inhabited, but after
, is buried upon the island of
, although no trace of his grave remains. He was the first European who is known to have explored the
. He was a navigator of enterprise, courage and tact, and although a Portuguese by birth, spent most of his life in the service of
About the middle of October 1542, just fifty years after
landed on the beach of what is now

, and was met by natives in their canoes, who received him with demonstrations of curiosity and kindness, and exchanged presents with him. He remained some time, exploring the surrounding country, which in his reports to
was described as of great attractiveness and fertility. He crossed to the islands of
and made the latter his headquarters, while he continued his explorations northward to a point many leagues north of the
. It is difficult to determine how far north he went, but we know from his narratives that he was driven back by bad weather and returned to
to recover from a fracture of his right arm, but he grew worse instead of better and on January 8, 1543, he died and was buried on
, another Portuguese epxlorer, who set sail from
with three large caravels and a transport, carrying 200 soldiers and three Carmelite friars.
, one of the friars, took copious notes of all they saw and heard along

, made a map of considerable detail and as directed by the explorer named each bay, cape, island and other conspicuous landmark after the particular saint whose festival occurred nearest to the day of discovery. On December 4, 1602, the party landed at
, and that being the anniversary of
, her name was given to the bay and the country that surrounds it.
was not the first discoverer of
, his voyage was the most important because of the maps of
and the other friars were able to furnish
in regard to that part of the world. The island of
recieved its name from the fact that one of the early explorers lost there a crucifix of great sanctity, which was found by the natives and restored as the vessel was about to sail.
A romantic narrative relates how in 18[5]1
, found human footprints upon the sandy beach of the island of
, which was supposed to be uninhabited. He was reminded of a story that had been widely circulated about

who had been abandoned there and made a thorough search. On the first day he wa unsuccessful; on the second day he found evidences of human existence, including a basket rudely made of reeds; on the [third] day he found three small huts [made] of the ribs of a whale and cove[red] with brush. In one of these huts [was]
, with long hair fa[ll]ing over her bare shoulders, and we[ar]ing a single garmet made of [the] skins of birds.
She showed no ala[rm] but when
, and his co[m]panions, approached she offered th[em] roots and a piece of blubber to [eat.]
She had entirely lost the faculty [of] speech.
told [her] that she was to accompany him to

[,] she understood read[ily,] showed much pleasure and gather[ed] together her little belongings, wh[ich] consisted of baskets woven of gra[ss,] jars filled with pebbles and other [little] mementoes. Upon reaching

took her to [his] home, where she was kindly cared [for] until her death three or four ye[ars] later. Although she was never able [to] express herself so as to be under[stood,] she had not forgotten the art of ho[use]keeping, and performed the duties [of] a servant as long as her health p[er]mitted.

to collect [all] the Indians that remained there [and] take them over to the missions. Wh[en] they were ready to depart

, [and] ran from the boat into the woods [...] search for her. Before her return [a] violent storm arose, and as there [was] no harbor and the anchorage was [...] secure, it became necessary to [...] out into the open sea[.]
, [...] for several days the ship was dr[iven] southward by the violence of the [gale] and finally landed its passengers [...]
started northward with a [...] of lumber for
was forgotten and re[main]ed for sixteen years alone, like Ro[bin]son Crusoe.
died shortly after the deser[tion,] from exposure to the weather.

was the official seat of the [gov]ernor, and the commandant at

, and of a promin[ent] family. He left a son,
, [who] became governor of the state, and [a] member of the house of repre[senta]tives. His family still reside in [the] neighborhood.
At the time of the arrival of
and the property [which] surrounded it were held by
, two Ameri[cans] under a lease from the

for a yearly rental of $1,[...] The lease covered all the lands [...] buildings of the missionary except [the] church and the monastery. Some li[...] head of cattle, 1,700 head of sheep [...] many horses were also included. [The] bishop of the
diocese [en]deavored in vain to resist the a[...] of the government, and his death [was] hastened by his failure. He died [in] 1846 and is buried in
, the lessee, was a [Cape] Cod sea captain who first visited
in command of a trading ve[ssel] in 1822, en route to the
, and was so much taken [with] the climate and surroundings of

, that he brought his [wife] and family and settled there in 1[...] starting a trading store, bought [...] and cattle and became the richest [man] in the community. Nicholas A. [...] an Irish physician, married H[ill's] daughter. He arrived in 1836 and [prac]ticed medicine while he raised ca[ttle.] Before his death he, too, had be[come] very rich and was the owner of [...] large ranches as well as 10,000 ca[ttle.]
Several of the residences of [the] Spanish and Mexican colonial p[...] are still standing, although they [have] been considerably altered on mo[...] lines. The best description of life [in]
at that period may [be] found in Dana's "Two Years Be[fore] the Mast," which in the days of [...] youth was a universally popular [...]
